sentences.text: Oh yes.
Well, at that time, I didn't actually distinguish from one neighbor to the other because everybody seemed to be pleasant in a way.
We didn't mingle too much with the neighbors.
We were actually-- there were two Jewish families in the entire building, which was a three-story building.
I don't remember exactly how many occupants.
Our apartment was number 45 on third floor.
But I don't recall exactly how many tenants were, so there must have been quite a few tenants in that building.
The only two tenants that-- actually three tenants, neighbors, that I recall-- which I got to know them later on when the war broke out much better.
But at that time, before the war, I remember, especially next door, one next-door neighbor.
His name was Dombrowski-- Pietro Dombrowski.
This family had-- they had twin children.
I believe they were about my age, maybe a year younger.
But they-- the boy was kind of a weakling-- I considered him.
We couldn't play with him.
So they didn't have much to do with my Gentile friends in the street.
I would see him as a next-door neighbor.
They would come from school.
But we never really played together.
And I know my parents had quite a, a friendship with them.
They used to invite them for, like, a gathering, coffee.
And occasionally, my mother would make some good pastry.
[CRYING] They would-- she would bring it in.
And they would have coffee.
And my mother would make a, a good vishniak, which is wine and cherries-- and she would share it with them.
So that-- I knew them, at that point, just from being friendly to us, more than some other neighbors.
Then we had another neighbor, which lived about four apartments farther.
His name was Sokolowski.
And he was a streetcar conductor.
Actually, he was an operator, one of the streetcar operators that ran Prague, Warsaw, on certain lines.
So he-- they had no children.
They were basically about the same age with my parents, maybe a little bit younger.
But they socialized with these two neighbors.
So I recall them very well, especially the one next door to us, Dombrowski, which-- they were good friends.
So as we go on later on in the war years, they helped me quite a bit.
Tell me what led up to the war years.
Well, as I can recall, in 1939, for example, my father was-- he was an invalid from World War I.
He had a glass eye and also had a, a scar on his hand-- I don't recall his right or his left hand.
And he was actually dreaming-- or maybe it was a reality-- that someday, as a invalid, he will be able to open a little tobacco stand or something like that.
That needed a, a license and some loan support from the government.
And they were saving money for this particular purpose, that someday they'll be able to do that.
And my father would not have to work in the factory-- he worked in a factory in the new town Praga, or they call it Nowa Praga.
And it was quite a distance for him to walk.
And I don't think he was able to afford a bicycle.
I remember he used to cross-- it was quite a bit of fields to walk between our building and the part that he worked.
He had to walk through fields and then cross over, like, an embankment of a railroad running.
I remember, I walked with him a couple times, and it was quite a distance to walk to his job.
My sister, being older than I, they sent away just before the war.
They sent her away to this camp.
And she came back after the summer-- which I missed her very much.
I really never told her I cared for her so much because I always tried to, to fight her and argue with her.
But not being with her one summer, I missed her quite a bit.
And when she came back, she told us-- she was more on the north side of Poland, somewhere-- that it was quite a buildup from soldiers over there.
She was telling us about this.
And then-- oh, I recall even a year prior to that-- that was 1938-- we had some friends.
Actually, my father knew these people that came from Germany.
They were exiled.
Or they ran away from Hitler.
And they came-- they stayed in our apartment-- in our house for a couple of days.
And they were telling horrible stories about Hitler, about the regime, which to me, it was something new.
Because I never paid any attention to news at that time.
I suppose I was too small.
But I was, at that time, already getting interested-- what's going on.
And when these people were telling the stories-- how the German people treated Jews and why they had to leave Germany.
They were on their way to, I believe, to China.
But they were coming through, like transit, through Poland.
Well, they told us that they were beaten and thrown out of their possessions, of their stores, and how other Jews were treated, beaten up, and their property was taken away from them.
So to me, it was a shock because I never knew what means being thrown out of a house, or being beaten up.
And this kind of mattered to me.
A policeman was the man that really protected the people.
But over there, the way they described that, the, the police came, and they threw them out.
So it was kind of-- I couldn't, couldn't quite understand what was really going on.
But I knew that Hitler-- the name Hitler wasn't too kind to Jews and, and seemed like something was cooking, something-- like, people would be scared of Germans, in particular.
And coming-- like my sister, when she came back afterwards, and telling that there might be a war with Germany-- so it kind of-- I was a, a little bit more aware of what was going on.
And we were frightened of this situation.
I was frightened of this situation as a child.
And I remember my father came once from work-- I don't know if it was work, or he had a meeting from this-- he belonged to the Bund, from the Jewish workers organization.
And he came, and he was quite beaten up.
And he had a broken nose and telling us that some of the hooligans, hooligans beat him up because he was a Jew.

So all that, the situation was getting tense and insecure for Jews to go out in the evening or trying to be more indoors and not to-- and they would kind of warn me not to run around too much and stay, more or less, near the house, things like that.
So that was kind of leading up to the war, which I didn't understand what, what actually war meant.
The only war that I knew-- that I was reading some small books.
I, I liked adventure.
And I read about World War I a little bit.
But I wasn't really that much interested in that.
So I can't recall too much until the actual fact of the-- of the war came in.
Like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I seen soldiers being concentrated in our neighborhood.
There was a lot of soldiers, Polish soldiers, camping out in the streets with their motorcycles, camping out in the park.
So actually, it wasn't the park-- it was the-- where we lived was quite a few empty lots over there.
So they were putting up their camps over there.
And there was, like, more and more soldiers in the-- in the neighborhoods.
So you could see some of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cars coming in with soldiers, and artillery with horses moving in because most of the time, the horses would pull the cannons, what I've seen.
To me, it was really exciting to see all that.
But they warned me that this is not fun, that this might be leading to a war, which I actually didn't understand what this whole thing meant.
The first day of war, I remember I was sitting and watching the window, looking out through the window.
And I've seen a, a plane in the sky.
And there was another, another plane chasing him.
And my father, at that time, actually was sitting by my next-door neighbor, Dombrowski.
And they were chatting about the war.
Everybody was talking about the war.
And I heard shooting in the sky from these two planes.
And when I came and I told that to the people, they thought I'm kind of fantasizing about the war.
But I know for sure that there was a-- like, a dogfight from planes in the sky.
And--
That was 1939.
That was actually the break of the-- of World War II in Poland.
So that was the beginning of the-- I was-- I remember the first-- the first time that I saw planes actually having a dogfight in the sky, as I can remember, and not fantasizing.
Because right after-- right after that, the radio announced that the war actually started.
And they started to bomb Warsaw.
So we had planes already coming over and bombing regular the city.
[SIGHS] At that time, my father thought it would be much safer for us to move to Warsaw-- leave everything behind and move to Warsaw where my uncle, at that time, lived on Panska.
I don't remember-- it was Panska 14.
It was a huge apartment complex, three-story buildings.
But, like, it had three courts.
The buildings were built-- the old buildings, like, you walked into the main gate.
And there was like an atrium, like a court, maybe 100 feet by 100 feet, something like that.
Then you had another gate.
And you walked in through a little space, and you walked into another court.
And there were like three courts like that.
So he lived in, in this complex over there, with these three courts.
I remember we came over to Warsaw.
And, and we stayed with them during the bombardment.
The bombardment was not constantly.
It was on and off.
And I remember we tried to cross the bridge, and the bridge was already bombed from the Germans.
It was most-- there were two bridges in Warsaw, that I can recall.
One was called Kierbedz, and one was Poniatowski.
One was a, a bridge-- a steel bridge-- a steel structure.
The other one was a concrete bridge, like an open bridge.
And we went through the steel bridge-- we crossed over the steel bridge.
I think that bridge was called Kierbedz.
I'm not sure.
I get confused between these two bridges which one was which.
But the steel bridge, we crossed by foot.
And actually, they stood-- you know, the Polish soldiers stood guard.
And they wouldn't let anybody on the bridge.
But my father introduced himself, that he was a, a Polish ex-soldier, and he was an invalid.
And he-- they-- somehow, they always gave him the, like, respect in that-- in that matter.
So when he showed them the documents, they would let us through.
We came through the bridge.
And we walked all the way to my uncle, which is-- his name is-- name was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe was Moishe.
I am not 100% sure.
It was Avram or Moishe.
I'm not sure.
I kind of got mixed up at this point.
We stayed during the bombardment-- I would say few weeks.
Because it continued-- the siege of Warsaw continued, I think, about three weeks.
And they bombed the city quite often.
So I remember we stood in his apartment over there-- in his house.
Every time they bombed it, we would run down to the cellar, which was a huge cellar.
Which was really stupid to sit in the cellar, because later on we found out that a lot of people sitting in the cellar got buried alive.
But at that point, we thought that this would be the safest place.
And the bombardments were quite, quite often.
And at night, we would come out from the cellar and look around.
I remember at one time, I came out from the cellar.
And all around us, everything was burning.
Warsaw was in fire-- was on fire.
And right after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bombardment stopped.
And we were actually able to come back to Praga, to-- back to our place.
I guess the, the war ended at that-- not the war ended, but the bombardment ended.
And they must have surrounded-- the city was surrounded.
And probably, they gave up fighting.
I don't know.
At that point, I don't remember.
But we were able to come back.
So we came back.
And we went to Praga.
And I remember we had an aunt living on Szeroka Street.
So we tried to find out if she was alive.
She was not with us at my uncle's.
A lot of my relatives came to my uncle, and we stayed there in the cellar.
When we came back, I remember there was a lot of dead horses laying in the streets.
And they were kind of swollen or blown-up.
I had never seen a horse so big laying in the street.
And there was quite a few of them, laying in the street like that.
We came back to our apartment.
The door was broken.
The door was knocked down.
And there was really not that much to be taken.
We didn't have that many things to be taken, except furniture, which they didn't take.
They were looking probably for some valuables, which-- my father probably took it with him.
So there was not too many things missing in the apartment.
We cleaned things up.
My father fixed the apartment.
And I don't remember how many days passed by.
But they announced that the Germans walked into Warsaw.
I remember when they-- first marching of German soldiers through the street of Radzyminska.
So they were walking in huge numbers.
They were marching like a parade, like I remember parades before the war that I used to go with my father to see-- a Polish parade.
But these were German soldiers.
And they looked very, very neat, with helmets and rifles and big boots.
And they marched through the streets.
I remember the, the German gendarmerie would march in front of them.
And they had over here, [POINTS] like, a, a big plaque-- a metal plaque in front on their chests.
They were field gendarmerie, or something like that.
And there was a lot of motorcycles with soldiers sitting in them.
And this was my first encounter with Germans.
So--
After that, things kind of quiet down.
And everybody started to, to go among his business, to do something, to try to gather food, and things like that.
And I remember my father was told that there was-- there was some food could be taken on the Wisla-- on the Vistula River.
There was some barges or ships standing there with food.
So I remember he went down there to get some food.
And he kind of twisted his ankle.
But he brought some food.
There was no, no stores open.
You couldn't get any food, or anything like that.
So he brought in some food from the barges.
And we started to, to eat.
I don't know how many days we didn't have any food, or if my parents prepared themselves, and they had some, some food.
We had dry food.
I don't remember being hungry or anything like that.
It was more excitement and fright than anything else for me.
So things got kind of back to normal.
And we went to school.
I remember-- we're supposed to go to school.
So I went to school the first day.
And I found out that the school that I attended was all burned up.
There was no school.
The school was-- there were barracks-- wooden barracks.
The school was completely charred.
There was nothing there.
The Polish kids went to school.
But we could not attend school.
They told us that they will let us know if we could get temporary school arrangements somewhere else.
But then, within a short time, the announcement came that the Jewish children would not be able to attend school.
There was no school for Jewish children.
That's as far as I can remember.
But the Polish children continued going to, to their school.
Oh, that-- it was coming in-- into the fall of 1939.
So we lived-- we tried to live back-- like, a normal life.
Everybody got back to his place, whoever had a place to come back to.
A lot of people didn't have any more shelter, any more housing, because a lot of houses were burned.
But luckily for us that we lived in Praga.
Praga was not destroyed that bad as Warsaw.
So we had our apartment.
Some of my sister's friends, I remember, had their apartments.
And we got to see each other.
My sister started to teach some Jewish kids.
I don't know where, in what place.
But I remember she used to go every day to her friend's.
And they would try to teach the smaller children.
But for some reason, I never got there.
I don't know if I didn't want to go there, or there was no room for me to go there.
I have no idea.
But I never attended any classes after that.
She tried to give me some lessons in the house.
And my father started to work as a glazier.
He was like a, a handyman.
Actually, he was an iron worker, and he was a locksmith.
But he was kind of versatile.
And he was good, almost like a jack-of-all-trades.
So he figured out that, at the time, fixing panes and windows would be the best deal that he could make because it was coming to winter.
And it was-- people had to close in their apartments.
So he got himself a big box, I remember, with glass.
And he had a diamond cutter that he used.
And I was helping him out.
He took that big box on his back, walking around.
And he would come just like a, a, you know, like a peddler, get over to the court of a building and start screaming, 'glazier' and 'glazier' in Polish.
And people would call him in and have him fix their windows.
So he was quite busy.
And I tried to help him.
And I even have a scar till today that I tried to cut a piece of glass, and the glass broke and probably cut my vein or something.
Because I couldn't-- I couldn't stop bleeding-- I couldn't stop bleeding from my-- from my finger.
But nevertheless, I think I did a pretty good job helping him.
So we used to get up in the morning and go and fix glass as much as we could.
Also, he was able to fix broken locks that people had that-- you know, people broke into their apartments.
And they had broken doors and broken locks.
So he would take care of the locks and the windows, and all that.
And we were able to buy food and eat from that.
So that continued through the winter.
Well, they continued pretty normal, as far as I'm-- I remember, until it got in into the summer of 1940.
Except that it was very dangerous for my father to walk in the streets.
Every once in awhile, the Germans would beat up Jewish men, especially older people.
The Germans, by themselves, did not identify the Jews that were not visual as Jews-- they did not look like Jews.
If any Jew wore a, a Jewish hat-- which they used to wear a black hat with a beard-- they would identify them as Jews.
But if a Jew did not wear that, they couldn't identify the Jew directly.
But the danger came in that every once in awhile, they would catch men to work.
And for some reason, the Polacks would help out the Germans catch these men.
They would identify the Jewish men walking the street.
My father had a hook nose, didn't speak Polish very well-- he spoke Polish well, but he had a Jewish accent.
So did my mother.
If you conversed with my father probably like two sentences, you could have told-- you could identify him that he was a Jew.

I don't remember exactly at what point they came up with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH], what they called it, that you had to wear the Jewish-- the Star of David, which was, in Warsaw, a white band with a blue [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sign of David, the Magen David.
So-- but I, I don't remember at what month they came out with this law that you had to wear it.
But I remember prior to that, my father was called few times to work.
But he always came back.
He always managed even to bring some food.
Somehow, when he was called to work, he would manage always to do something kind of good for the Germans, like fix them-- locks or doors.
I don't know.
But instead of coming-- like some other people were beaten up from this trip-- he would come with some reward, with some food.
So somehow, he, he managed to, to survive in a pretty good way.
And this continued through the summer of 1940, or so.
We-- my mother was very good in selling things.
She was kind of also very helpful.
She would buy bread and sell it on the-- on the market.
We had a little stand.
OK.
OK.
OK.
